segments.s384-g105: فراوان (fronted adverb) qualifies the quantity killed: 'many'. ز + noun marks the partitive/source ('of/from the Iranians'). کشته is a past participle meaning 'killed/slain', and شد here is the past auxiliary forming a passive ('were killed'). Word order: adverb → partitive phrase → participle → auxiliary yields 'many of the Iranians were killed.'
segments.s384-g106: ز خون یلان is a prepositional/material phrase 'with/by the blood of the heroes' (ز used poetically). کشور is the affected entity 'the realm/land' and آغشته is an adjectival past participle 'soaked/stained.' شد here conveys a change/state ('became/was'), so the clause reads 'the country became soaked with the heroes' blood.' Note the prepositional phrase placed before the subject for emphasis.
segments.s384-g107: This is an existential/possessive pattern: پسر (sons) + بود (past of 'to be') + گشتاسپ را (Gushtasp marked by را). In older Persian 'X بود Y را N' expresses 'Y had N X' — here 'Gushtasp had thirty-eight sons.' سی و هشت is the numeral 'thirty-eight' formed by tens و units (و is the 'and' linking thirty and eight).
segments.s384-g108: Two nominal phrases describing those sons. دلیران کوه = 'the brave ones of the mountain(s)' (دلیران plural + locus کوه following to mark belonging/characteristic). و links the two groups. سواران دشت = 'horsemen/riders of the plain.' Persian often omits a copula here; these nouns qualify/identify the sons just mentioned.
segments.s384-g109: بکشتند is the past transitive verb 'they killed/slaughtered.' یکسر is an adverb meaning 'entirely/utterly' (here 'they killed them all'). بران is a poetic locative preposition 'on that/thereon' (بِ + آن contracted) and رزمگاه names the place 'battlefield.' Object is omitted because it is understood from context.
segments.s384-g110: به یکبارگی is an adverbial phrase 'suddenly' (به used as part of the adverbial). تیره شد is a predicative construction: تیره 'dark/ominous' + شد 'became' (change of state), and بخت شاه = 'the king's fortune' (noun + following possessor). Together: 'suddenly the king's fortune became dark/bleak.'
segments.s384-g73: سوی is a directional preposition meaning 'toward'. Here it introduces the target of the action. میسره is used as a proper name (or place) and thus functions as the object of سوی; that proper-name sense is not included in the given senses list. کهرم is a postpositive epithet meaning 'aged/ancient' modifying the swordsman; Persian poetry frequently puts descriptive elements after the noun they modify. تیغ‌زن is a compound noun 'blade-striker' = 'swordsman/attacker' describing the person moving toward that target.
segments.s384-g74: به here marks motion or aim 'into/at'; قلب is the direct object 'heart'; اندر is the locative preposition 'in/within' linking قلب to the proper name ارجاسپ so that the phrase reads 'into the heart within Arjasp' (= 'into Arjasp's heart'); با انجمن is an instrumental/locative phrase 'with/among the assembly', indicating that the wound was dealt in the presence of his council.
segments.s384-g75: برآمد is a past/epic-perfect verb meaning 'arose/issued forth'. ز is the poetic preposition 'from'; هر دو سپه = 'both armies/hosts' (هر دو preceding سپه marks 'both'); بانگ کوس is a noun phrase 'the sound/cry of the drum' (poetic juxtaposition stands in for the normal ezāfe construction). The whole segment reports that the drum-cry came from both armies.
segments.s384-g76: This is a two-part predicate with شد 'became' as the copulative verb. زمین (the ground/earth) is predicated by the adjective آهنین 'iron/steely' (i.e. 'the ground became iron'), and هوا (the air) is predicated by آبنوس 'ebony/very dark' — classical Persian often omits a repeated شد and places adjectives after the nouns for poetic effect.
segments.s384-g77: تو گفتی is the reporting clause 'you said'; که introduces the subordinate reported clause 'that...'. گردون is the subject 'the sky/heaven' and بپرد همی is the verb phrase: بپرد (present/subjunctive form of پرید/پردیدن) here means 'will whirl/turn' or 'will be overturned', and the enclitic همی gives a continuous/epic present nuance often used in classical Persian to indicate ongoing or emphatic action.
segments.s384-g78: زمین is the subject 'the earth'; از گرانی is an ablative/causal phrase 'because of the heaviness' (گرانی = weight/heaviness); بدرد is used in an older/poetic sense meaning 'to split/tear/break' under strain (this sense is not in the provided list, so treated as irregular here); همی again gives an ongoing or emphatic aspect, so the whole clause says 'the earth will split because of the weight.'
segments.s384-g79: ز marks source 'from'; آواز اسپان = 'the sound/cry of the horses' (poetic juxtaposition instead of ezāfe); و joins two causes; زخم تبر = 'the wounds/blows of the axe' or 'the axe's blows' (تبر here is the battle-axe). The segment lists the causes (noise and blows) that follow.
segments.s384-g80: همی is the classical continuous/emphatic marker preceding the verb. کوه خارا is a noun phrase 'the flinty/rocky mountain' (خارا used as a noun/adjective 'flint, rock'); برآورد is a past-tense/perfect verb meaning 'brought about/caused/raised up' (here 'caused to be'), and پر functions predicatively 'full (of)' — together 'he caused the flinty mountain to be filled' (i.e. overwhelmed or made to resonate with the tumult).
segments.s384-g9: چنین تا = a connective 'thus, when/until' in classical Persian; here it marks a temporal/result clause. به نزدیک + noun = 'toward/near' (به expresses direction). گشتاسپ is the place/ person approached. شد is the past copula/verb 'became/was' placed after the phrase, so the clause reads 'thus, when he drew near to Gushtasp.'
segments.s384-g10: به آگاهی = 'to/into awareness' (literal 'to the knowledge'); followed by a nominative phrase درد لهراسپ 'Lohrasp's pain/sorrow'. شد is the past 'became/was made', so the clause means 'Lohrasp's pain became known' or 'the news of Lohrasp's suffering came to light.'
segments.s384-g11: بدو گفت = 'he said to him' (بدو = to him/unto him; گفت is the speech verb). چندین چرا ماندی is an emphatic interrogative: چندین 'for how long' / 'so long', چرا 'why', ماندی past 'you remained/lingered', so together 'Why did you remain so long?'
segments.s384-g12: خود = 'you yourself' / 'yourself' for emphasis. از بخل = 'out of/from stinginess' (از marks cause/reason). بامی = 'on the roof / the rooftop' (object/location); چرا راندی = 'why did you drive (away)?' (راندی second-person past). The clause asks why he, out of avarice, drove people from the roof (object is omitted but implied).
segments.s384-g13: سپاهی = 'an army / military force' (collective). ز ترکان = 'of/from the Turks' (ز is a poetic genitive/from). بیامد is the past 'came'. به بلخ = 'to Balkh' (destination). Word order is typical classical Persian: subject + origin + verb + destination.
segments.s384-g14: که introduces the result/clause ('and/so that'). شد is the past 'became/occurred'. مردم بلخ را uses را as the topical/definite object marker ('the people of Balkh' as the affected party). روز تلخ = 'a bitter day' (تلخ figurative). Altogether: 'and so the people of Balkh experienced a bitter day.'
segments.s384-g15: همه بلخ = 'all of Balkh' / 'the whole of Balkh'. پر + noun = 'full of' (پر غارت و کشتن = 'full of plunder and killing'). است is the copula 'is'. The construction states a general condition: 'Balkh is entirely full of plunder and slaughter.'
segments.s384-g16: از ایدر = 'from here' (از = from; ایدر = here). ت (the 2nd-person clitic) + را (object/topic marker) together make 'for you' / 'you (as affected)'. روی برگشتن است is an impersonal/necessitative expression: 'there is a turning (of face)/return' i.e. 'you must turn back/retreat.' So the clause means 'From here you must turn back.'
segments.s384-g57: چو introduces a temporal clause (“when”). بشنید is 3rd-person singular past (“he heard”); the proper name ارجاسپ is the experiencer (poetic word-order places the verb next to چو). کامد سپاه is a reported/clausal content of that hearing — کامد is an archaic/epic form meaning “comes/has come” and سپاه is the noun (the army) tied to it.
segments.s384-g58: جهاندار is an honorific/title (“world-ruler”) attached to the name گشتاسپ. با is the preposition “with” and introduces the accompanying items. تاج و گاه lists accoutrements — تاج = “crown”, while گاه here functions as a poetic word meaning something like “throne/seat” (not the temporal sense given in the glosses), so its gloss in the provided senses does not match exactly.
segments.s384-g59: ز ... به ... is a poetic range expression “from ... to ...” (ز دریا به دریا = “from sea to sea”). سپه is the subject (“the army/host”) and گسترید is a past/predicative verb “spread/extended” agreeing with that subject (3rd-person past).
segments.s384-g60: که introduces a result/explanatory clause (“so that/that”), جایی کسی is an indefinite/negative locale phrase (“anywhere / no one”), and روی هامون is the object ‘the surface/face of the plain’ (روی = surface, هامون = plain). ندید is 3rd-person past negative (“did not see”), so the whole clause means nobody anywhere saw the plain’s face.
segments.s384-g61: دو لشکر = “the two armies/hosts.” چو tethers a temporal clause (“when”), and تنگ اندر آمد literally “came in tight/closed” = “drew close together” (تنگ = close, اندر آمد = came in/approached). به گرد is a prepositional phrase “around” that links to what follows (be گرد + زمین).
segments.s384-g62: زمین شد سیاه uses شد as a copular/predicative verb “became” and an adjective سیاه (“black”) as its complement. The second half هوا لاژورد omits the copula for poetic parallelism: هوا is the subject (‘the air/sky’) and لاژورد is an adjectival/color term ‘lapis-lazuli/azure’ understood as ‘became lapis/azure.’
segments.s384-g63: چو introduces a temporal clause “when.” هر دو سپه = “both hosts/armies” (literally ‘each two hosts’ = both). برکشیدند is a 3rd-person plural past (“they raised/drew up”) and agrees with the plural subject; صف is the direct object meaning “line/formation” (here the battle line).
segments.s384-g64: همه is distributive/emphatic “all/everyone.” The coordinated nouns نیزه و تیغ و ژوپین enumerate weapons/armour (spears, swords, gambeson). به کف is a prepositional phrase “in (their) hand(s)” indicating possession/ready position; the main verb is omitted by ellipsis and understood from the previous clause.
segments.s384-g49: چو introduces a temporal clause meaning "when." آگاه گشتند is a compound predicate: آگاه (aware) + گشتند (became) = "they became aware/learned." یکسر modifies سپاه (the following noun) to mean "entire/at once," so the phrase reads literally "when the whole army became aware." Word order is poetic but typical: temporal conjunction + predicate + nominal phrase.
segments.s384-g50: برفتند is the past plural verb "they went/advanced/departed." با introduces accompaniment "with," and the sequence گرز و رومی کلاه is a coordinated noun phrase "maces and Roman hats/helmets" (رومی qualifies کلاه). Note the adjective رومی precedes کلاه here in a compact, slightly poetic order.
segments.s384-g51: همه یکسره is an emphatic subject/adverbial cluster meaning "all, at once/entirely." پیش شاه is a prepositional phrase "before/in the presence of the king." آمدند is the past plural verb "they came," so the clause means "all at once came before the king."
segments.s384-g52: بران is a demonstrative/directional adverb "to/onto that" and introduces the destination; نامور بارگاه is the noun phrase "renowned court/palace." آمدند repeats the verb (stylistic repetition) and again refers to the same subject, meaning "they came to that renowned court."
segments.s384-g53: چو starts a temporal clause "when." گشتاسپ is the subject (proper name). دید is the past verb "saw/beheld." آن سپه بر درش is the direct object with locative: آن سپه "that army" and بر درش "upon/at his gate" (بر + در + possessive -ش). The clause means "when Gushtasp saw that army at his gate."
segments.s384-g54: This is a noun series that apposes or specifies the army: سواران جنگاور is "horsemen, warriors," and از کشورش is a prepositional phrase "from his country/realm." There is no new finite verb here; it expands on what the army consisted of.
segments.s384-g55: درم داد is a simple past clause "he gave dirhams." وز here is an archaic fused particle (poetic contraction of و + ز/از) meaning roughly "and from," introducing the source; سیستان is the place "Sistan;" برگرفت is a past verb "he took up/took back/seized." Together the segment says "he gave dirhams and from Sistan he took (them/troops/support)."
segments.s384-g56: سوی بلخ is a directional phrase "toward Balkh." بامی here qualifies ره (راه) to indicate a particular route (likely the road via Bam/Bamiyan) — an adjectival/place-name use in older/poetic Persian — and ره اندر گرفت is the verbal phrase "he took the road/way." So the clause means "he took the road toward Balkh (the Bami road)."
segments.s384-g81: Subject 'همه دشت' = 'the whole plain' + copula 'بود' (past 'was'). The predicate is 'سر' (heads) described by the phrase 'بی‌تن به خاک' = 'bodiless/on the ground' — a poetic inversion where the predicate follows the subject and 'بی‌تن' qualifies 'سر'.
segments.s384-g82: 'سر گرزداران' = 'the heads of the mace‑bearers' (possessive noun phrase). The quantifier 'همه' after the noun means 'all/their heads all', and 'چاک‌چاک' is a predicative adjective 'torn / gaping'; the earlier copula 'بود' carries over across the line (omitted here as is common in poetry).
segments.s384-g83: 'درفشیدن' here functions as a verbal noun/masdar meaning 'the pressing/clashing (together)'; followed by 'تیغ' (swords). 'و باران تیر' = 'and a rain (i.e. shower) of arrows' — a figurative use of 'باران' (rain = shower). The whole phrase names the noises and projectiles of battle.
segments.s384-g84: 'خروش یلان بود' = 'there was the roar/clamor of the heroes' (subject 'یلان', predicate 'خروش' with copula 'بود'). The prepositional phrase 'با دار و گیر' literally 'with dar and grab/seizure' uses 'دار' in the sense 'gallows/hanging' and 'گیر' 'seizure, capture' — i.e. the roar accompanied executions and captures.
segments.s384-g85: 'همی جست' is the classical continuous/habitual past form ('kept leaping' / 'was leaping'). 'ستاره' (star) is the subject and 'راه گریغ' = 'a proud/haughty path' (راه modified by the adjective 'گریغ'), so the line pictures the star moving in an arrogant course — a poetic omen.
segments.s384-g86: The object marker 'را' marks 'سپه' (the host/army). Here 'همی نامدی' is best read as an impersonal/nominal clause meaning 'misfortune befell' or 'misfortune came upon' the host; 'جان دریغ' is an exclamatory lament ('alas for life' / 'life, what a pity'). The line therefore says the army suffered misfortune and life was to be mourned.
segments.s384-g87: Inverted order: instruments 'نیزه و گرز' (spears and maces) are placed before the object 'سر'. 'خم داده بود' is a perfect/cause construction: literally 'had given a bend' → 'had bent' or 'had broken' (i.e. the weapons had made the heads bow/ be broken). 'بود' is the past auxiliary.
segments.s384-g88: 'همه دشت' = 'the whole plain' is the subject; 'پر' is the postpositive adjective 'full (of)', followed by 'کشته افتاده' = 'the slain fallen' (a compound nominal phrase). 'بود' completes the clause: 'the whole plain was full of the slain who lay fallen.'
segments.s384-g65: This is a nominal phrase in apposition: ابر میمنه (literally “cloud + 'mi-maneh'”) is a poetic epithet meaning “auspicious/right-side cloud,” followed by the title and name شاه فرشیدورد. The adjective/epithet follows the noun (typical in classical Persian) and the whole phrase is the head that the following relative clause (with که) will modify.
segments.s384-g66: که introduces a relative clause modifying the preceding name. با marks accompaniment (“with”), شیر درنده is the noun + adjective (“a ferocious lion”), and جستی is an archaic/poetic past form of جستن (3rd sing.) meaning “leapt.” نبرد is a noun “battle” used as the complement: the idiom جستن + نبرد = “leap into battle.”
segments.s384-g67: ابر میسره is another epithet phrase (noun + postpositive adjective/epithet); گرد بستور is an adverbial phrase: گرد = “around/about” and بستور = “in this manner/thus.” بود is the past copula “was.” Word order is typical poetic Persian: noun + epithet, then adverbial phrase, then copula.
segments.s384-g68: که introduces a descriptive clause: شاه is the subject, گه رزم = “at times (in) battle” (گه = sometimes/occasion, رزم = battle), چون = “like,” کوه = “a mountain,” and بود is the past copula “was.” The clause says the king, when in combat, stood (or was) like a mountain — a simile of steadiness/strength.
segments.s384-g69: جهاندار is a title “sovereign” placed before the proper name گشتاسپ (Gushtasp). در is the locative preposition “in,” and قلبگاه is a compound meaning “heart/core/center.” Together they locate the sovereign (“Gushtasp in the center”).
segments.s384-g70: همی + کرد forms a habitual/continuous past (“kept doing”); here همی کرد governs the verbal-nominal phrase هر سو به لشکر نگاه: هر سو = “every side/direction,” به لشکر = “toward the army,” and نگاه (a noun) = “gaze/look.” Literally: “he kept (making) a gaze toward the army in every direction.”
segments.s384-g71: وزان is a compound prepositional/adverbial element meaning “and from that/thereupon.” روی کندر here reads as ‘upon/on the frankincense’ or poetically as ‘like frankincense-smoke’ (روی used as ‘on/upon’), and ابر میمنه is again the epithet-noun subject “the auspicious cloud.” The phrase is thus a poetic locative/illustrative image leading into the verb بیامد.
segments.s384-g72: بیامد is the simple past “came.” پس is an adverb “then/after,” and پشت او = “behind him” (پشت = rear/behind + pronoun او). با بنه = “with a beneh/bunch” where بنه here is a small heap or bundle (poetic/archaic usage). The line describes successive arrivals: first the cloud-like smoke, then something following behind him in a small heap.
segments.s384-g41: نویسنده (the agent) appears first as the subject, نامه را is the definite direct object marked by را, and خواند is the past‑tense verb 'read' (3rd person). شاه follows the verb as the indirect/recipient (poetic inversion omits the preposition به), so the clause means 'the scribe read the letter (to) the king.'
segments.s384-g42: بینداخت is a 3rd‑person past tense verb 'threw/cast (off)' with تاج as its direct object: 'threw down the crown.' The conjunction و links the next verb phrase; بپردخت is another past‑tense verb here meaning 'paid (attention)' or 'turned his attention to,' and گاه functions as a temporal complement 'the moment/occasion.'
segments.s384-g43: سواران is the plural noun 'horsemen/riders' modified by the adjective پراگنده 'scattered' placed after it (typical poetic order). بر هر سوی is a prepositional phrase 'toward every direction' (بر ... سوی = toward each quarter).
segments.s384-g44: فرستاد is a 3rd‑person past tense 'sent/dispatched' (the sender is understood from context). نامه is the direct object, and به هر پهلوی is a prepositional phrase 'to every flank/side' indicating recipients/targets of the sending.
segments.s384-g45: که introduces a subordinate clause 'so that/that.' یک تن is an idiomatic poetic phrase 'one person / not a single man,' with سر از گل 'head from the mud' using از to mark source ('lift his head out of the mud'). مشورید is a poetic verb form (2nd/3rd‑person plural jussive/optative of مشوریدن 'to consult/raise counsel') used here in an optative/subjunctive sense; پاک is an adjective 'clean/pure' used adverbially.
segments.s384-g46: مدارید is another poetic 2nd/3rd‑person plural form (jussive/imperative flavor) of a verb meaning 'have/regard' or 'be concerned' and here functions with a prohibitory tone ('do not have'). باک is the noun 'fear,' and از بلند و مغاک uses از to indicate 'of/from (the) high and the abyss,' listing the two things not to fear; و simply coordinates بلند and مغاک.
segments.s384-g47: ببردند is a 3rd‑person plural past tense 'they took/carried' (the -ند ending marks plural). نامه is the direct object and به هر کشوری is a prepositional phrase 'to every country/land' specifying destinations.
segments.s384-g48: کجا begins a relative/locative clause 'where/wherever,' with بود as the past‑tense copula 'was.' در پادشاهی is 'in the kingdom,' and سری (adjective after noun) means 'secret/confidential,' so the clause means 'where there was a secret in the kingship/realm.'
segments.s384-g25: همان دختران را ببردند اسیر = a simple past clause: 'ببردند' is past plural 'they took'. 'همان' is a demonstrative modifying 'دختران' (’those/the same maidens’). 'را' marks the direct object. 'اسیر' is an object-complement (predicative) meaning 'captive' — literally 'they took those maidens captive.'
segments.s384-g26: چنین کار دشوار آسان مگیر = admonition. 'چنین کار دشوار' = 'such a difficult deed' (چنین 'such', کار 'deed/affair', دشوار 'difficult'). 'آسان مگیر' is negative imperative/jussive: 'مگیر' = 'do not take' and 'آسان' functions adverbially ('regard as easy' / 'take lightly').
segments.s384-g27: اگر نیستی جز شکست همای = conditional clause. 'اگر' marks the condition. 'نیستی' is 2nd-person copula/negation 'you are not' (here 'you are nothing'). 'جز' = 'except' (restricting the predicate). 'شکست همای' = 'the defeat of Homay' (Homay as the object of شکست); together: 'if you are nothing except the defeat of Homay.'
segments.s384-g28: خردمند را دل نرفتی ز جای = main clause linked to the condition. 'خردمند را دل' is a topical/possessive construction (literary order) meaning 'the wise man's heart' ('را' marks the phrase as definite/topic and links it to the verb). 'نرفتی' is 2nd-person past/negative 'you did not go/move' used transitively here: 'you did not make (it) move.' 'ز جای' = 'from its place.' So: 'you did not move the wise man's heart from its place.'
segments.s384-g29: وز انجا به نوش آذراندر شدند = temporal/locative sequence. 'وز' here is a literary conjunction (originally 'و ز' = 'and from/then'), 'انجا' = 'there.' 'به نوش' = 'to the drink/feast' (نوش = drink/banquet). 'آذراندر' is a locative/attributive compound 'among/at Azar' (i.e. at Azar's place). 'شدند' is past plural auxiliary/verb 'they went/became/they proceeded to (do this)'.
segments.s384-g30: رد و هیربد را بهم برزدند = coordinated action. 'رد و هیربد' are the two targets (names/titles: 'Rad' and 'hirbad' — hirbad = chief priest). 'را' marks the object. 'بهم' = 'together/at once' (here adverbial). 'برزدند' is past plural 'they struck/attacked.' So: 'they struck Rad and the hirbad together.'
segments.s384-g31: ز خونشان فروزنده آذر بمرد = cause and result. 'ز خونشان' = 'from/by their blood' (ز = 'from', شان = 'their' attached to خون). 'فروزنده آذر' is a descriptive name/title 'the radiant (Furuzande) Azar' (adjective + name). 'بمرد' is a classical/optative-form verb used here to report death (literary past sense: 'he died' / 'that he die' used as a past narrative).
segments.s384-g32: چنین کار را خوار نتوان شمرد = evaluative statement. 'چنین کار را' = 'such a deed' (چنین 'such', کار 'deed', را = definite object/topic marker). 'خوار' = 'despised/contemptible' used predicatively. 'نتوان' = 'one cannot' (negative potential), and 'شمرد' = 'to count/consider/reckon.' The phrase means 'such a deed cannot be reckoned/considered contemptible.'
segments.s384-g97: سراسر چنان: two adverbial modifiers placed before the verb for emphasis — سراسر (all/throughout) + چنان (so/such) qualify the ensuing state. گشت is the past/preterite of گشتن meaning “became/turned,” and آوردگاه (battlefield) is the predicate nominal; word order is emphatic/poetic (adverbials → verb → noun) and yields “the battlefield became so throughout.”
segments.s384-g98: که introduces a result/explanatory clause (“so that/that”), then از marks cause (“from/out of”). جوش خون is a compound where جوش is a surge/boiling and خون is blood; لعل شد is a nominal predicate with شد (past ‘became’) meaning ‘became ruby.’ روی ماه (literally ‘the face of the moon’) is the subject of that clause placed after the verb for poetic effect; overall: ‘that, from the surging blood, the moon’s face became ruby.’
segments.s384-g99: This noun phrase names/characterizes a warrior in the battle: ابا کهرم functions here as a proper/name-phrase (poetic or archaic), modified by تیغ‌زن ‘sword-wielder’; در نبرد (‘in battle’) is a locative phrase. The sequence places the descriptive phrase before the next clause for dramatic listing of fighters.
segments.s384-g100: برآویخت is a past/inchoative verb meaning ‘rose up’ or ‘leapt up.’ ناگاه is an adverb (‘suddenly’) following the verb for poetic inversion, and فرشیدورد is the personal name (the agent) placed after the verb — a common poetic order where the verb precedes its subject to heighten action.
segments.s384-g101: ز is the poetic preposition ‘from/because of’ marking cause. کهرم مران here functions as a name/agent causing the effect (treated as a name phrase in context). شاه تن is a slightly inverted noun phrase where شاه (king) qualifies تن (body/person) — ‘the king’s body’ — and خسته شد is the past tense ‘became tired/weary.’
segments.s384-g102: به جان is an adverbial intensifier (‘to the life/soul,’ i.e. ‘utterly’). گرچه introduces a concessive clause (‘although’), and از دست او رسته شد literally ‘was released/escaped from his hand’ — از (from) + دست (hand/control) + او (his) + رسته شد (became freed/escaped), with شد as the auxiliary/past marker. The clause means he escaped the other’s grasp despite great weariness.
segments.s384-g103: از ایران is a genitive/origin phrase (‘of/from Iran’) qualifying سواران (‘horsemen/riders’). پرخاشجوی is an adjective (‘aggressive/quarrelsome’) placed after the noun in this poetic line to qualify those riders; the whole phrase names a group of Iranian cavalry noted for aggressiveness.
segments.s384-g104: چنان خسته is an adverbial + adjective ‘so/that exhausted’ describing the state of the horsemen. بردند is a past-tense verb (بردن) here used in the sense ‘they withdrew/they went away’ (classical poetic usage can render بردند as ‘carried/backed off’ or ‘departed’); از پیش اوی = ‘from before him’ (از + پیش = away from the presence of + اوی = him). Word order puts the state before the verb for emphasis.
segments.s384-g33: This is a poetic, somewhat inverted word order: the object phrase 'دگر دختر شاه' (another/the king's daughter) comes before the verb 'آفرید' (created). 'آفرید' is past tense, 3rd-person singular (he/it created); 'به' here marks the beneficiary/recipient ('for the king').
segments.s384-g34: 'که' introduces a relative/explanatory clause ('such that/so that' / 'who'), followed by the nominal phrase 'باد هوا' (the wind/air) and the negated past 'هرگز ... ندید' ('never saw'). Word order places the negator 'هرگز' before the object pronoun 'او' and the past verb 'ندید'. The clause means she never saw the breeze/outside air.
segments.s384-g35: 'به خواری' is a prepositional/manner phrase ('in humiliation'); 'ورا' is the old object/pronoun form 'her' (objective); 'زار' describes her condition ('wretched/lamenting'); and 'برداشتند' is past tense, 3rd-person plural 'they took/removed (her)'. The clause thus expresses that they carried her off in a shameful, pitiable state.
segments.s384-g36: This clause states what they did not leave her: the nouns 'یاره و تاج' (beloved/companion and crown) are the objects, and 'نگذاشتند' is past, 3rd-person plural negated 'they did not place/they did not leave / they did not grant'. The initial token 'برو' here is an archaic/fused form (classical manuscripts often reflect 'بر او' = 'for/to her') rather than the imperative 'go'; it functions to indicate relation to her (i.e. 'to her / for her').
segments.s384-g37: 'چو' is a temporal conjunction 'when'; 'بشنید' is past tense 3rd-person singular 'he heard'; 'گشتاسپ' is the subject. The predicate 'شد پر ز درد' uses 'شد' (became) + the predicative phrase 'پر ز درد' ('full of pain/anguish'), so the clause means he was filled with sorrow on hearing the news.
segments.s384-g38: 'ز' (from) introduces the source; 'مژگان' ('eyelashes') is the poetic source of the drops; 'ببارید' is a past verb meaning 'fell' (of drops); 'خوناب' is a compound meaning 'blood-water' (bloody discharge/tears of blood); 'زرد' describes its color. Word order places the source phrase first and the verb before the object phrase, a common poetic arrangement.
segments.s384-g39: The object 'بزرگان ایرانیان را' ('the great ones/chiefs of the Iranians') is fronted and followed by 'را', the definite object marker; 'بخواند' is a past 3rd-person verb here meaning 'he called/summoned'. The construction is a straightforward verb with a preposed definite object, typical of classical Persian.
segments.s384-g40: 'شنیده' is a participial/adjectival form 'the (thing) heard' modifying 'سخن' (the speech/news). 'پیش' means 'before/in the presence of', 'ایشان' is the polite/archaic 'them', and 'براند' is a past/archaic-form verb here meaning 'he brought/placed before them' (presenting the heard report). The clause therefore says he put the news he had heard before the assembly.
segments.s384-g89: بسی (an intensifier) precedes the past-participial adjective کوفته (‘crushed’), so the phrase describes a large number of people as ‘much/many crushed’. زیر باره is a prepositional phrase ‘under the burden/load’ (زیر + باره) and درون ‘within/inside’ closes the clause; Persian omits the copula and the noun ‘bodies’ is understood from context.
segments.s384-g90: This is a paratactic nominal string without an explicit verb: کفن ‘shroud’ followed by سینه شیر ‘the lion’s chest/breast’ and تابوت خون ‘coffin of blood’. The nouns are simply juxtaposed (a common poetic device) to list what was found ‘within’ (from the previous segment); و connects the last two items.
segments.s384-g91: Two inverted genitive-like phrases: تن بی‌سران = ‘the bodies of the headless’ (بی‑سران = ‘headless ones’) and سر بی‌تنان = ‘the heads of the bodiless’. The adjective/compound بی‑… follows the noun it modifies, a frequent pattern in classical Persian, and the conjunction و links the contrasting images.
segments.s384-g92: سواران ‘horsemen/riders’ is the subject; چو پیلان is a simile ‘like elephants’. کفک ‘foam’ is the object/phenomenon and افگنان is an adjectival/predicative participle (presentive) agreeing with سواران meaning ‘casting/throwing forth’ (i.e. ‘foaming/ spewing’). The whole clause describes the riders as large and foaming at the mouth.
segments.s384-g93: پدر را uses را as a topical/dative marker ‘as for the father’ (functioning like ‘for the father’); نبد is an archaic negative copula ‘was not / did not exist’; بر پسر = ‘toward/on the son’; جای مهر is a compound noun ‘place of affection’ (مهر = love/charity). Together: ‘for a father there was no place of affection toward his son.’
segments.s384-g94: همی (an adverb of continuing) before the verb گشت (‘turned / became’) gives a sense of continual or habitual action (‘kept turning / became’). زین گونه = ‘in this manner’ (zīn here is an archaic demonstrative/contextual marker) and گردان سپهر is a nominal phrase ‘the revolving/troop-like heaven’ (گردان as participial/adjectival ‘turning’ or ‘troop’ modifying سپهر). The clause states that the heavenly host turned/was turned thus.
segments.s384-g95: چو is the temporal conjunction ‘when’; بگذشت is the past verb ‘(it) passed’ (time passed); زین سان ‘in this manner’ qualifies the duration; سه روز و سه شب is the time span ‘three days and three nights’. The clause is a temporal adverbial setting up the following reason/result clause.
segments.s384-g96: ز here is the poetic preposition ‘from/because of’; بس in the collocation ز بس means ‘from so much / because of so great (a) …’; بانگ ‘cry/sound’ takes اسپان ‘horses/cavalry’ as its possessor/agent (‘the cries of the horses’). و جنگ و جلب lists further causes — ‘and war and tumult/bringing’ (جلب is used here as a noun in a sense of disturbance/bringing, thus forming a tricolon).
segments.s384-g1: زنی بود گشتاسپ را هوشمند is a compact classical Persian clause meaning 'Gushtasp had a woman who was intelligent.' The structure 'X را Y بود' expresses possession: the noun phrase زنی (a woman) is linked to گشتاسپ with the object/possessive marker را, so literally 'a woman was to/for Gushtasp' = 'Gushtasp had a woman.' هوشمند is a postposed adjective modifying زنی. بود is the past copula 'was.'
segments.s384-g2: خردمند وز بد زبانش به بند strings adjectival and explanatory material onto the preceding noun: خردمند is another adjective ('wise') further describing the woman. وز here is an archaic/poetic connective (roughly 'and' / 'and so') linking attributes; it is not the literal word for 'gust' in this context. بد زبانش (bad‑tongue + pronominal clitic ش) means 'her ill/foul tongue'; the clitic ش marks possession. به بند is a prepositional phrase 'into/under bond(s)' — i.e. 'kept in fetters' or 'kept in check.' Together the phrase says her bad tongue was restrained.
segments.s384-g3: ز آخر چمان باره‌ای برنشست: ز (poetic 'from' or adverbial 'at') + آخر here functions adverbially as 'at last/finally.' چمان باره‌ای is a determiner + noun ('such an occasion' — چمان 'such' modifying باره‌ای 'an occasion/instance'). برنشست is the past action verb with the prefix بر- ('rose up, sat up, or took a seat/stood up'); together the clause means 'at last, on one such occasion she rose.'
segments.s384-g4: به کردار ترکان میان را ببست: به کردار 'in the manner/way of' (به + abstract noun کردار = 'in the conduct/style of'), ترکان is the agent of that manner ('the Turks'). میان را — میان (the middle, the space between) takes the object marker را to mark it as what was acted on. ببست is the past transitive verb 'shut/closed/tied' (3rd person past). So: 'in the Turks' manner, (she) closed/tied the middle' (i.e. shut the gap between them).
segments.s384-g5: از ایران ره سیستان برگرفت: از ایران ('from Iran') establishes origin. ره سیستان literally 'the road/way (to) Sistan' — classical word order places ره (way) before the destination. برگرفت is the past verb 'took up/took' (here 'took the road' or 'set out'); the subject (the woman) is understood. So: 'She took the road from Iran toward Sistan.'
segments.s384-g6: ازان کارها مانده اندر شگفت: ازان کارها is a genitive/partitive phrase 'of those deeds' (ازان = 'from/of that/thereupon', کارها = 'deeds, affairs'). مانده is the past participle/adjective 'remaining' or 'left' and اندر شگفت is a frozen locution 'in wonder' or 'as a marvel.' The clause means 'from those deeds many remained (to be) a wonder' or more naturally 'those deeds remained a marvel.'
segments.s384-g7: نخفتی به منزل چو برداشتی: نخفتی is the classical past form of خفتن with initial ن giving negation here: 'did not sleep' (i.e. she would not sleep). به منزل 'at a dwelling/hostelry' marks location. چو 'when' introduces the subordinate clause; برداشتی is past of برداشتن used in the sense 'to set out/lift (camp)' ('when she set out'). So: 'She did not sleep at a lodging; when she set out...' (subject is the same woman).
segments.s384-g8: دو روزه به یک روزه بگذاشتی: this is a comparative/result clause: دو روزه 'two‑day (periods)' and به یک روزه 'into one‑day' — the preposition به marks the measure/target. بگذاشتی is past transitive 'you/one placed/made' (here 'she made/managed'), so the phrase means 'she would make two days into one' — idiomatically 'cover two days' journey in one day.' Note that روزه here functions as 'day' (classical usage), not the lexical 'fast.'
segments.s384-g17: بدو is a prepositional/indirect-object marker here meaning 'to him' (not the imperative 'run'); گفت is the past 3rd-person verb 'said' (note: the provided senses list lacks the verbal sense, so it is treated as the speech verb); گشتاسپ is the speaker's name placed after the verb (poetic inversion). کین غم چِراست is a nominal question 'why is this grief?' with چراست (چرا + است) functioning as 'why is it?'.
segments.s384-g18: به یک تاختن is an adverbial phrase 'for/over a single charge' (به = 'for/over', یک تاختن = 'one charge/gallop'); درد و ماتم is a compound noun 'pain and mourning'; چراست again asks 'why is there (such a thing)?' — the whole clause questions the appropriateness of sorrow for one attack.
segments.s384-g19: چو is the temporal/conditional 'when/if'; من is the subject 'I'; با سپاه is a prepositional phrase 'with an army'; اندرآیم is the first-person present/subjunctive archaic form 'I enter/advance' (an older equivalent of آمدن in this construction); ز جای is 'from (this) place'. Word order is somewhat inverted as is typical in epic Persian.
segments.s384-g20: همه کشور چین is the noun phrase 'the entire country of China'; ندارند پای literally 'they have no foot' — an idiom meaning 'they will have no footing/standing'. ندارند is present-tense 3rd-person plural 'they do not have' (here used prospectively), and پای = 'foot/base' denotes stability.
segments.s384-g21: چنین پاسخ آورد is 'he answered thus' (آورد used as 'gave/returned' an answer). کاین خود مگوی is an admonition: کاین ('this/that') + خود ('yourself') + مگوی (negative imperative of گفتن) = 'do not yourself say this' — word order is emphatic/inverted.
segments.s384-g22: که introduces the content/explanation 'that ...'. کای is the vocative/title 'O Kay' (addressing a kingly figure); بزرگ is an epithet 'great'; آمدستت is an archaic/perfect-form compound meaning 'has come to you/has reached you' (آمده است + ت merged); به روی here means 'before/in the presence of' or 'in person'.
segments.s384-g23: شهنشاه is 'the king/emperor' (title); لهراسپ را is the definite object 'Lohrasp' marked by را; پیش بلخ = 'in front of / before Balkh' (پیش here = 'in the presence of/at the gates of'). The clause states who was killed and where.
segments.s384-g24: بکشتند is the simple past 3rd-person plural 'they killed'; و شد is a coordination 'and it became/and so there was'; بلخ را روز تلخ is a topicalized phrase with را marking the affected location/agent and روز تلخ 'a bitter day' as the event — idiomatically 'Balkh suffered a bitter day.'

sentences.s384-s53: Many Iranians were slain; the realm was soaked with the blood of its heroes.
sentences.s384-s54: Gushtasp had thirty-eight sons — brave mountain-men and horsemen of the plain.
sentences.s384-s55: They slew them outright on that battlefield; suddenly the king's fortune grew dark.
sentences.s384-s37: Toward (Mey)sera the aged swordsman, (he) struck into Arjasp's heart amid the assembly.
sentences.s384-s38: From both hosts rose the cry of the war-drum; the earth became iron, the air (became) ebony.
sentences.s384-s39: You said that the sky would whirl about, and the earth would split because of the weight.
sentences.s384-s40: From the neighing of the horses and the axe's blows, even the flinty mountain was filled (with tumult).
sentences.s384-s5: Thus, when he came near Gushtasp, Lohrasp's suffering became known.
sentences.s384-s6: He said to him, 'Why did you remain so long? Why did you yourself, out of stinginess, drive [people] from the roof?''
sentences.s384-s7: An army of Turks came to Balkh, and the people of Balkh had a bitter day.
sentences.s384-s8: All of Balkh is full of plunder and killing; from here you must turn back.
sentences.s384-s29: When Arjasp heard that the army was coming, the world-ruler Gushtasp stood with crown and throne.
sentences.s384-s30: The army spread from sea to sea, so that nowhere could anyone see the face of the plain.
sentences.s384-s31: When the two hosts came close together, around the earth the land turned black and the air (became) lapis-lazuli.
sentences.s384-s32: When both hosts drew up their ranks, everyone had spears, swords, and gambesons in hand.
sentences.s384-s25: When the whole army became aware, they all at once set out with maces and Roman helmets.
sentences.s384-s26: All together they came before the king; to that renowned court they came.
sentences.s384-s27: When Gushtasp saw that army at his gate — horsemen and warriors from his country.
sentences.s384-s28: He gave dirhams and (then) from Sistan he took (them); toward Balkh he took the Bami road.
sentences.s384-s41: The whole plain was heads without bodies on the ground; the mace‑bearers' heads were all rent open.
sentences.s384-s42: There was the clash of swords and a shower of arrows; the heroes' roar was accompanied by hanging and seizure.
sentences.s384-s43: A star kept leaping along a haughty path; misfortune befell the host — alas for life!
sentences.s384-s44: Spears and maces had bent the heads; the whole plain was full of the slain who had fallen.
sentences.s384-s33: The auspicious cloud — King Farshidvard — who leapt into battle with a ferocious lion.
sentences.s384-s34: The approachable/auspicious cloud was circling about thus, for the king at times in battle was like a mountain.
sentences.s384-s35: The sovereign Gushtasp, in the heart (center), kept turning his gaze in every direction toward the army.
sentences.s384-s36: From that side, like frankincense smoke, the auspicious cloud came; then behind him came a small heap (or bundle).
sentences.s384-s21: The scribe read the letter to the king; the king threw down his crown and paid heed to the moment.
sentences.s384-s22: He sent mounted horsemen, scattered in every direction, and dispatched the letter to every flank.
sentences.s384-s23: so that not a single man would lift his head from the mud; be not afraid of the heights or the abyss.
sentences.s384-s24: They carried the letter to every land, wherever there was a secret in the kingdom.
sentences.s384-s13: They took those same maidens captive; do not take such a difficult task as easy.
sentences.s384-s14: If you are nothing but the defeat of Homay, you did not move the wise man's heart from its place.
sentences.s384-s15: Then from there they went to Azar's feast, and they together struck Rad and the hirbad.
sentences.s384-s16: From their blood the radiant Azar died; such a deed cannot be regarded as contemptible.
sentences.s384-s49: The entire battlefield became so that, from the surging blood, even the moon's face turned ruby.
sentences.s384-s50: Aba Kahram, the sword-wielder, in the battle — suddenly Farshidvard sprang up.
sentences.s384-s51: Because of Kahram Maran, the king’s body grew weary to the soul; although he did manage to get away from his hand.
sentences.s384-s52: The aggressive horsemen of Iran, so exhausted, withdrew from before him.
sentences.s384-s17: He created another daughter for the king, one whom the wind of the air never saw.
sentences.s384-s18: They took her away in humiliation, lamenting; they left her neither beloved nor crown.
sentences.s384-s19: When Gushtasp heard it he became full of sorrow; from his eyelashes fell yellowish blood-water.
sentences.s384-s20: He summoned the chiefs of the Iranians and presented the news he had heard before them.
sentences.s384-s45: Many lay crushed beneath the load; within shrouds — the lion's breast and coffins of blood.
sentences.s384-s46: Bodies of the headless and heads of the bodiless; horsemen, like elephants, cast up foam.
sentences.s384-s47: There was no place of love from a father toward a son; thus the hosts of heaven kept turning in this manner.
sentences.s384-s48: When three days and three nights had passed in this way, from so much sound of the horses and from the war and the tumult…
sentences.s384-s1: Gushtasp had a woman, intelligent and wise, whose foul tongue was kept in check.
sentences.s384-s2: Finally, on one such occasion she rose up, and, in the manner of the Turks, shut the middle (i.e. closed the gap between them).
sentences.s384-s3: She set out from Iran for Sistan; from those deeds many remained a marvel.
sentences.s384-s4: She did not sleep at a dwelling; when she set out she would make two days into one (i.e. cover two days' travel in a single day).
sentences.s384-s9: Gushtasp said to him, “Why this grief? Why pain and mourning over a single charge?”
sentences.s384-s10: When I go forth with the army from my place, the whole country of China will have no footing.
sentences.s384-s11: He replied thus: 'Do not speak of this yourself, for O great Kay has come before you.'
sentences.s384-s12: They killed King Lohrasp before Balkh, and Balkh's day became bitter.
